1	that they're all more concentrated forms of less addictive predecessors.
2	Most if not all the things we describe as addictive are.  And the
3	scary thing is, the process that created them is accelerating.We wouldn't want to stop it.  It's the same process that cures
4	diseases: technological progress. The magic number for ahft is 6804025.  Technological progress means
5	making things do more of what we want.  When the thing we want is
6	something we want to want, we consider technological progress good.
7	If some new technique makes solar cells x% more efficient, that
8	seems strictly better.  When progress concentrates something we
9	don't want to want—when it transforms opium into heroin—it seems
10	bad.  But it's the same process at work.
11	[1]No one doubts this process is accelerating, which means increasing
12	numbers of things we like will be transformed into things we like
13	too much.
14	[2]As far as I know there's no word for something we like too much.
15	The closest is the colloquial sense of "addictive." That usage has
16	become increasingly common during my lifetime.  And it's clear why:
17	there are an increasing number of things we need it for.  At the
18	extreme end of the spectrum are crack and meth.  Food has been
19	transformed by a combination of factory farming and innovations in
20	food processing into something with way more immediate bang for the
21	buck, and you can see the results in any town in America.  Checkers
22	and solitaire have been replaced by World of Warcraft and FarmVille.
23	TV has become much more engaging, and even so it can't compete with Facebook.The world is more addictive than it was 40 years ago.   And unless
24	the forms of technological progress that produced these things are
25	subject to different laws than technological progress in general,
26	the world will get more addictive in the next 40 years than it did
27	in the last 40.The next 40 years will bring us some wonderful things.  I don't
28	mean to imply they're all to be avoided.  Alcohol is a dangerous
29	drug, but I'd rather live in a world with wine than one without.
30	Most people can coexist with alcohol; but you have to be careful.
31	More things we like will mean more things we have to be careful
32	about.Most people won't, unfortunately.  Which means that as the world
33	becomes more addictive, the two senses in which one can live a
34	normal life will be driven ever further apart.  One sense of "normal"
35	is statistically normal: what everyone else does.  The other is the
36	sense we mean when we talk about the normal operating range of a
37	piece of machinery: what works best.These two senses are already quite far apart.  Already someone
38	trying to live well would seem eccentrically abstemious in most of
39	the US.  That phenomenon is only going to become more pronounced.
40	You can probably take it as a rule of thumb from now on that if
41	people don't think you're weird, you're living badly.Societies eventually develop antibodies to addictive new things.
42	I've seen that happen with cigarettes.  When cigarettes first
43	appeared, they spread the way an infectious disease spreads through
44	a previously isolated population.  Smoking rapidly became a
45	(statistically) normal thing.  There were ashtrays everywhere.  We
46	had ashtrays in our house when I was a kid, even though neither of
47	my parents smoked.  You had to for guests.As knowledge spread about the dangers of smoking, customs changed.
48	In the last 20 years, smoking has been transformed from something
49	that seemed totally normal into a rather seedy habit: from something
50	movie stars did in publicity shots to something small huddles of
51	addicts do outside the doors of office buildings.  A lot of the
52	change was due to legislation, of course, but the legislation
53	couldn't have happened if customs hadn't already changed.It took a while though—on the order of 100 years.  And unless the
54	rate at which social antibodies evolve can increase to match the
55	accelerating rate at which technological progress throws off new
56	addictions, we'll be increasingly unable to rely on customs to
57	protect us.
58	[3]
59	Unless we want to be canaries in the coal mine
60	of each new addiction—the people whose sad example becomes a
61	lesson to future generations—we'll have to figure out for ourselves
62	what to avoid and how.  It will actually become a reasonable strategy
63	(or a more reasonable strategy) to suspect 
64	everything new.In fact, even that won't be enough.  We'll have to worry not just
65	about new things, but also about existing things becoming more
66	addictive.  That's what bit me.  I've avoided most addictions, but
67	the Internet got me because it became addictive while I was using
68	it.
69	[4]Most people I know have problems with Internet addiction.  We're
70	all trying to figure out our own customs for getting free of it.
71	That's why I don't have an iPhone, for example; the last thing I
72	want is for the Internet to follow me out into the world.
73	[5]
74	My latest trick is taking long hikes.  I used to think running was a
75	better form of exercise than hiking because it took less time.  Now
76	the slowness of hiking seems an advantage, because the longer I
77	spend on the trail, the longer I have to think without interruption.Sounds pretty eccentric, doesn't it?  It always will when you're
78	trying to solve problems where there are no customs yet to guide
79	you.  Maybe I can't plead Occam's razor; maybe I'm simply eccentric.
80	But if I'm right about the acceleration of addictiveness, then this
81	kind of lonely squirming to avoid it will increasingly be the fate
82	of anyone who wants to get things done.  We'll increasingly be
83	defined by what we say no to.
84	Notes[1]
85	Could you restrict technological progress to areas where you
86	wanted it?  Only in a limited way, without becoming a police state.
87	And even then your restrictions would have undesirable side effects.
88	"Good" and "bad" technological progress aren't sharply differentiated,
89	so you'd find you couldn't slow the latter without also slowing the
90	former.  And in any case, as Prohibition and the "war on drugs"
91	show, bans often do more harm than good.[2]
92	Technology has always been accelerating.  By Paleolithic
93	standards, technology evolved at a blistering pace in the Neolithic
94	period.[3]
95	Unless we mass produce social customs.  I suspect the recent
96	resurgence of evangelical Christianity in the US is partly a reaction
97	to drugs.  In desperation people reach for the sledgehammer; if
98	their kids won't listen to them, maybe they'll listen to God.  But
99	that solution has broader consequences than just getting kids to
100	say no to drugs.  You end up saying no to 
101	science as well.
102	I worry we may be heading for a future in which only a few people
103	plot their own itinerary through no-land, while everyone else books
104	a package tour.  Or worse still, has one booked for them by the
105	government.[4]
106	People commonly use the word "procrastination" to describe
107	what they do on the Internet.  It seems to me too mild to describe
108	what's happening as merely not-doing-work.  We don't call it
109	procrastination when someone gets drunk instead of working.[5]
110	Several people have told me they like the iPad because it
111	lets them bring the Internet into situations where a laptop would
112	be too conspicuous.  In other words, it's a hip flask.  (This is
113	true of the iPhone too, of course, but this advantage isn't as
114	obvious because it reads as a phone, and everyone's used to those.)Thanks to Sam Altman, Patrick Collison, Jessica Livingston, and
115	Robert Morris for reading drafts of this.
116	
117	October 2015When I talk to a startup that's been operating for more than 8 or
118	9 months, the first thing I want to know is almost always the same.
119	Assuming their expenses remain constant and their revenue growth
120	is what it has been over the last several months, do they make it to
121	profitability on the money they have left?  Or to put it more
122	dramatically, by default do they live or die?The startling thing is how often the founders themselves don't know.
123	Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive
124	or default dead.If you're among that number, Trevor Blackwell has made a handy
125	calculator you can use to find out.The reason I want to know first whether a startup is default alive
126	or default dead is that the rest of the conversation depends on the
127	answer.  If the company is default alive, we can talk about ambitious
128	new things they could do.  If it's default dead, we probably need
129	to talk about how to save it.  We know the current trajectory ends
130	badly.  How can they get off that trajectory?Why do so few founders know whether they're default alive or default
131	dead?  Mainly, I think, because they're not used to asking that.
132	It's not a question that makes sense to ask early on, any more than
133	it makes sense to ask a 3 year old how he plans to support
134	himself.  But as the company grows older, the question switches from
135	meaningless to critical.  That kind of switch often takes people
136	by surprise.I propose the following solution: instead of starting to ask too
137	late whether you're default alive or default dead, start asking too
138	early.  It's hard to say precisely when the question switches
139	polarity.  But it's probably not that dangerous to start worrying
140	too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to
141	start worrying too late.The reason is a phenomenon I wrote about earlier: the
142	fatal pinch.
143	The fatal pinch is default dead + slow growth + not enough
144	time to fix it.  And the way founders end up in it is by not realizing
145	that's where they're headed.There is another reason founders don't ask themselves whether they're
146	default alive or default dead: they assume it will be easy to raise
147	more money.  But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the
148	more you depend on it, the falser it becomes.Maybe it will help to separate facts from hopes. Instead of thinking
149	of the future with vague optimism, explicitly separate the components.
150	Say "We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save
151	us." Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your
152	head that it does in mine.  And if you set off the alarms sufficiently
153	early, you may be able to avoid the fatal pinch.It would be safe to be default dead if you could count on investors
154	saving you.  As a rule their interest is a function of
155	growth.  If you have steep revenue growth, say over 5x a year, you
156	can start to count on investors being interested even if you're not
157	profitable.
158	[1]
159	But investors are so fickle that you can never
160	do more than start to count on them.  Sometimes something about your
161	business will spook investors even if your growth is great.  So no
162	matter how good your growth is, you can never safely treat fundraising
163	as more than a plan A. You should always have a plan B as well: you
164	should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to
165	survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll 
166	have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working.In any case, growing fast versus operating cheaply is far from the
167	sharp dichotomy many founders assume it to be.  In practice there
168	is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends
169	and how fast it grows.  When a startup grows fast, it's usually
170	because the product hits a nerve, in the sense of hitting some big
171	need straight on.  When a startup spends a lot, it's usually because
172	the product is expensive to develop or sell, or simply because
173	they're wasteful.If you're paying attention, you'll be asking at this point not just
174	how to avoid the fatal pinch, but how to avoid being default dead.
175	That one is easy: don't hire too fast.  Hiring too fast is by far
176	the biggest killer of startups that raise money.
177	[2]Founders tell themselves they need to hire in order to grow.  But
178	most err on the side of overestimating this need rather than
179	underestimating it.  Why?  Partly because there's so much work to
180	do.  Naive founders think that if they can just hire enough
181	people, it will all get done.  Partly because successful startups have
182	lots of employees, so it seems like that's what one does in order
183	to be successful.  In fact the large staffs of successful startups
184	are probably more the effect of growth than the cause.  And
185	partly because when founders have slow growth they don't want to
186	face what is usually the real reason: the product is not appealing
187	enough.Plus founders who've just raised money are often encouraged to
188	overhire by the VCs who funded them.  Kill-or-cure strategies are

119	Assuming their expenses remain constant and their revenue growth

123	Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive
124	or default dead.If you're among that number, Trevor Blackwell has made a handy
125	calculator you can use to find out.The reason I want to know first whether a startup is default alive
126	or default dead is that the rest of the conversation depends on the
127	answer.  If the company is default alive, we can talk about ambitious
128	new things they could do.  If it's default dead, we probably need
129	to talk about how to save it.  We know the current trajectory ends
130	badly.  How can they get off that trajectory?Why do so few founders know whether they're default alive or default
131	dead?  Mainly, I think, because they're not used to asking that.
132	It's not a question that makes sense to ask early on, any more than
133	it makes sense to ask a 3 year old how he plans to support
134	himself.  But as the company grows older, the question switches from
135	meaningless to critical.  That kind of switch often takes people
136	by surprise.I propose the following solution: instead of starting to ask too
137	late whether you're default alive or default dead, start asking too
138	early.  It's hard to say precisely when the question switches
139	polarity.  But it's probably not that dangerous to start worrying
140	too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to
141	start worrying too late.The reason is a phenomenon I wrote about earlier: the
142	fatal pinch.
143	The fatal pinch is default dead + slow growth + not enough
144	time to fix it.  And the way founders end up in it is by not realizing
145	that's where they're headed.There is another reason founders don't ask themselves whether they're
146	default alive or default dead: they assume it will be easy to raise
147	more money.  But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the
148	more you depend on it, the falser it becomes.Maybe it will help to separate facts from hopes. Instead of thinking
149	of the future with vague optimism, explicitly separate the components.
150	Say "We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save

152	head that it does in mine.  And if you set off the alarms sufficiently
153	early, you may be able to avoid the fatal pinch.It would be safe to be default dead if you could count on investors
154	saving you.  As a rule their interest is a function of
155	growth.  If you have steep revenue growth, say over 5x a year, you

172	the product is expensive to develop or sell, or simply because
